infobox.value: ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Ymddiriedolaeth Brifysgol GIG Gwasanaethau Ambiwlans Cymru*), or simply the **Welsh Ambulance Service**, is the national ambulance service for [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). As of April 2025, it has 3,500 staff providing ambulance and related services to the 3 million residents of Wales.[[3]](#cite_note-aboutus-3)
["The Welsh Ambulance Services National Health Service Trust (Establishment) Order 1998"](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/1998/678/2024-04-01). *legislation.gov.uk*. Retrieved 8 April 2025.

Hough, Lois (28 March 2024). ["Welsh Ambulance Service awarded University Trust status"](https://ambulance.nhs.wales/news/nhs/2024/welsh-ambulance-service-awarded-university-trust-status/). *Welsh Ambulance Service* (Press release).
["Contact Us - Welsh Ambulance Services University NHS Trust"](https://ambulance.nhs.wales/contact-us/). *ambulance.nhs.wales*. Retrieved 7 April 2025.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

NHS trust and ambulance service in Wales

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Welsh_Ambulance_Service "Special:EditPage/Welsh Ambulance Service") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Welsh Ambulance Service"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Welsh+Ambulance+Service%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Welsh Ambulance Services University NHS Trust | |
| --- | --- |
| Ymddiriedolaeth Brifysgol GIG Gwasanaethau Ambiwlans Cymru | |
|  | |
| Area covered by Welsh Ambulance Service | |

| Established | 1 April 1998 |

| Website | [ambulance.nhs.wales](https://ambulance.nhs.wales) [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q7981848#P856 "Edit this at Wikidata") |

The **Welsh Ambulance Services University NHS Trust** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Ymddiriedolaeth Brifysgol GIG Gwasanaethau Ambiwlans Cymru*), or simply the **Welsh Ambulance Service**, is the national ambulance service for [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). As of April 2025, it has 3,500 staff providing ambulance and related services to the 3 million residents of Wales.[[3]](#cite_note-aboutus-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_Ambulance_Service&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The trust was established on 1 April 1998,[[4]](#cite_note-4) following the *All Wales Ambulance Review*, recommending that the four existing ambulance trusts merge to create one service. This also included ambulance services provided by Pembrokeshire and Derwent NHS Trust.[[5]](#cite_note-WASTreview-5)

The trust was awarded "university" status by the Welsh government on 1 April 2024, making it the second ambulance trust to achieve university status in the United Kingdom.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_Ambulance_Service&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organisation")]

The trust headquarters are located at [St Asaph](/wiki/St_Asaph "St Asaph"). The service is currently divided into three regions:[[7]](#cite_note-7)

* Central and West Region – regional office at [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea")
* North Region – regional office at [St Asaph](/wiki/St_Asaph "St Asaph")
* South East Region – regional office at [Cwmbran](/wiki/Cwmbran "Cwmbran")

## Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_Ambulance_Service&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Services")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Mercedes_Benz_Sprinter_-_54681832919.jpg/250px-Mercedes_Benz_Sprinter_-_54681832919.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mercedes_Benz_Sprinter_-_54681832919.jpg)

A Welsh Ambulance Service [Mercedes-Benz Sprinter](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Sprinter "Mercedes-Benz Sprinter") ambulance in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff")

The trust provides a number of services:

Emergency Medical Services (EMS) - responds to emergency 999 calls and GP's urgent calls. A standard crew combination for this service would normally consist of a [paramedic](/wiki/Paramedic "Paramedic") and an [emergency medical technician](/wiki/Emergency_medical_technician "Emergency medical technician"). However double paramedic / double technician crews are not uncommon. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Welsh_Ambulance_Service&action=edit), the majority of the EMS fleet consists of WAS Mercedes Benz 519 Sprinter Ambulances and [Honda CRV](/wiki/Honda_CRV "Honda CRV") or Toyota RAV4 rapid response vehicles (RRV).

Non Emergency Patient Transport Service (NEPTS) - deals with the planned care aspect of ambulance work. NEPTS staff usually provide transport between home and healthcare facilities or some inter-hospital transfers.

Urgent Care Service (UCS) - bridges the gap between NEPTS and EMS, allowing for patients to be transferred between home and hospital or hospital to hospital while meeting the advanced needs that some of these patients may have (such as oxygen administration and continuous monitoring). UCS ambulance crews may also be allocated to EMS calls at times of high demand and following clinical telephony triage by a nurse or face to face triage by advanced paramedic practitioners or paramedic practitioners working from a RRV.

NHS Direct Wales / 111 Wales - a 24-hour telephone and internet health advice service provided by NHS Wales to enable people to obtain advice when use of the national emergency telephone number (999 or 112) does not seem to be appropriate but there is some degree of urgency. NHS Direct Wales / 111 Wales supports EMS Operations by providing clinical triage for "Green 3" calls that are deemed suitable. More than 45% of 999 calls have a disposition of not requiring 999 conveyance. In addition during times of escalation other calls deemed suitable are triaged. It does not replace any of the existing emergency or non-emergency medical services but complements those already existing and enables callers who might not be fully able to diagnose themselves to be directed to care of an appropriate level of urgency, including transport to hospital if the diagnosis merits that action.

Community First Responders (CFR) - volunteers from the community trained in basic first aid, oxygen administration and the use of an [automated external defibrillator](/wiki/Automated_external_defibrillator "Automated external defibrillator") (AED). They are used by the ambulance service mostly in rural areas to provide basic care, such as [cardiopulmonary resuscitation](/wiki/Cardiopulmonary_resuscitation "Cardiopulmonary resuscitation") (CPR) before an EMS crew arrives. As a CFR is usually only sent to local calls in specified communities, they often arrive before an EMS ambulance crew, even without the use of blue lights and sirens. Whilst most CFR teams are the sole responsibility of WAST, a number of teams (especially in the county of [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys")) are made up of regular divisions from [St John Ambulance Cymru](/wiki/St_John_Ambulance_Cymru "St John Ambulance Cymru") although this does not give them any exemptions.

There are developing numbers of advanced paramedic practitioners in the service who through their extended scope of practice are working toward advancing the service their patients receive with "see and treat" and "see and refer" models of care. This removes the need for some patients to travel in an ambulance to [A&E](/wiki/Emergency_department "Emergency department").

In 2012, a strategic review of the service was commissioned by the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") and was conducted by Professor Siobhan McClelland and published in April 2013.[[5]](#cite_note-WASTreview-5)

* [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales"), the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")

* [Wales Air Ambulance](/wiki/Wales_Air_Ambulance "Wales Air Ambulance")
